coredata.dc:title: B, N co-doped carbon nanosheets derived from graphene quantum dots: Improving the pseudocapacitive performance by efficient trapping nitrogen
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jing
Dong, Yue
Zhu, Jiayao
Wang, Luxiang
Tian, Wenhui
Zhao, Jing
Lin, He
Zhang, Su
Cao, Yali
Song, Huaihe
Jia, Dianzeng
coredata.dc:description: Doping heteroatoms especially nitrogen is widely applied for improving the capacitive energy storage performance of carbon materials. However, preserving nitrogen in carbon skeleton is still challenging because nitrogen species are unstable at high temperature. Herein, we develop a simple boric acid-assisted method for trapping nitrogen into carbon skeleton. Using the graphene quantum dots with an initial nitrogen content of 5.0 at.% as precursors, the B, N co-doped carbon nanosheets with an increased nitrogen content of 7.2 at.%, a boron content of 4.4 at.%, and a surface area of 817 m2 g−1 are prepared. Benefiting from the high heteroatom content, the nanosheets show a significantly improved capacitance of 257 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and a good rate performance of 150 F g−1 at 50 A g−1. The pseudocapacitance contribution at 10 mV s−1 is as high as 48%. The assembled symmetric capacitor shows a superior cycle stability with no obvious fading after 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1. Our work can help to understand the synergistic effect of heteroatoms for co-doping of carbon and broaden the avenue for designing functional carbon materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Doped carbon
Supercapacitor
High content
Trapping nitrogen
Pseudocapacitance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation routes of BN-CNSs and CNSs. The negatively charged nitrogen atoms of BN-CNSs can be partially neutralized by the positively charged boron atoms.
SEM images of (a, b) BN-CNSs and (c, d) CNSs. HRTEM images of (e) BN-CNSs and (f) CNSs.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) Raman spectra, (c) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, and (d) pore size distributions of CNSs and BN-CNSs.

(a) XPS survey, (b) C1s, and (c) N1s spectra of GQD precursor, CNSs, and BN-CNSs. (d) Scheme of the nitrogen-containing functional groups. (e) The content of various nitrogen components. (f) XPS B1s spectrum of BN-CNSs. (g) Nitrogen content evolution of our work compared with common nitrogen-containing carbon precursors after pyrolysis, activation, or NH3 treatment.

Electrochemical performance measured by three-electrode system: (a) CV curves at 10 mV s−1 and (b) galvanostatic charge-discharge curves at 1 A g−1 of CNSs and BN-CNSs. (c) CV curves at various scan rates and (d) galvanostatic charge-discharge curves at various current densities of BN-CNSs, (e) rate performance, (f) pseudocapacitance contribution based on the Trasatti analysis, (g) Nyquist plots, and (h) Bode plots of CNSs and BN-CNSs.

Electrochemical performance of two-electrode symmetric supercapacitors: (a) CV curves at 10 mV s−1 of CNSs and BN-CNSs. (b) Galvanostatic charge–discharge curves at various current densities of BN-CNSs. (c) IR drop, (d) rate performance of CNSs and BN-CNSs. (e) Comparison of the power performances of BN-CNSs, CNSs, activated carbon, and some of the previously reported carbon electrode materials. (f) Cycle performance at 10 A g−1 of CNSs and BN-CNSs.
Texture properties of CNSs and BN-CNSs measured by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: specific surface area calculated by the BET method.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Relative concentration of C, O, N, and B from XPS survey spectra, and contribution of the components from the area of the XPS N1s spectra.

row.entry: Contribution of components from N1s spectra (%)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: (N1: Pyridinic N, N2: C-N-B, N3: Pyrrolic N, N4: Graphitic N, N5: Pyridine-N-oxide).

head.ce:title: B, N co-doped carbon nanosheets derived from graphene quantum dots: Improving the pseudocapacitive performance by efficient trapping nitrogen

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Energy Materials Chemistry, Ministry of Education; Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials, Autonomous Region; Institute of Applied Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830046, PR China

Ministry of Education; Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resources Engineering, Beijing Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Process and Technology for Materials, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resources Engineering, Beijing Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Process and Technology for Materials, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: B, N co-doped carbon nanosheets prepared by boric acid-assisted carbonization show an increased nitrogen content than the precursor graphene quantum dots, enabling high pseudocapacitance for supercapacitor electrodes.

Doping heteroatoms especially nitrogen is widely applied for improving the capacitive energy storage performance of carbon materials. However, preserving nitrogen in carbon skeleton is still challenging because nitrogen species are unstable at high temperature. Herein, we develop a simple boric acid-assisted method for trapping nitrogen into carbon skeleton. Using the graphene quantum dots with an initial nitrogen content of 5.0 at.% as precursors, the B, N co-doped carbon nanosheets with an increased nitrogen content of 7.2 at.%, a boron content of 4.4 at.%, and a surface area of 817 m2 g−1 are prepared. Benefiting from the high heteroatom content, the nanosheets show a significantly improved capacitance of 257 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and a good rate performance of 150 F g−1 at 50 A g−1. The pseudocapacitance contribution at 10 mV s−1 is as high as 48%. The assembled symmetric capacitor shows a superior cycle stability with no obvious fading after 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1. Our work can help to understand the synergistic effect of heteroatoms for co-doping of carbon and broaden the avenue for designing functional carbon materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors have attracted great attention as next-generation energy storage devices because of the high power density, superior lifetime, and good safety. Carbon materials are widely used for supercapacitors due to the large surface area and good conductivity [1,2]. But the unsatisfied capacitance mainly coming from the electrical double-layer behavior still hinders their practical applications. To improve the capacitance, various pseudocapacitive components such as metal oxides and conducting polymers have been composited with carbon materials [2–4]. However, the poor rate performance and cycle stability of most pseudocapacitive materials severely deviate from the fast and stable energy storage feature of supercapacitors. By contrast, heteroatom doping of carbon materials can provide additional pseudocapacitance but without sacrificing the fast and stable energy storage ability, thus has attracted great research attention. Particularly, nitrogen-doping has been mostly investigated because of the high electronegativity and excellent compatibility towards carbon skeleton [2,5–7].

Two main routes are commonly used for preparing nitrogen-doped carbons: direct pyrolysis of nitrogen-containing precursors [8–10] and post-treatment with nitrogen-containing reagents [11–13]. But both of them confront with a severe issue of low nitrogen content because of the unstable feature of nitrogen-containing functional groups at high treating temperature. For example, nitrogen-containing polymers such as polyacrylonitrile [9,14,15], polydopamine [10,16,17], and polyaniline [8,18–20] are commonly used for preparing nitrogen-doped carbons. But gradual decomposition and release of nitrogen species during pyrolysis result in a much lower nitrogen content (<4 at.%) in the final productions than that in the precursors (>20 at.%). In addition, pyrolytic carbon usually has a low specific surface area [20,21]. Thus physical or chemical activation is usually needed to increase the surface area, further resulting in the loss of nitrogen during severe chemical etching [22–24]. Therefore, effective preserving of nitrogen species in doped carbon materials still remains a big challenge.

To increase the nitrogen content, several novel strategies have been developed to trap nitrogen into doped carbon materials, such as pyrolysis nitrogen-containing precursors in a confined space [25–27]; co-pyrolysis with nitrogen-containing molecules [28–31]; and introduce active sites to facilitate nitrogen-doping [32,33]. For example, through pyrolyzing polyacrylonitrile in confined SiO2 shells, nitrogen-doped hollow carbon spheres have been prepared with a high nitrogen content of 8.08 wt% [25]. Liu et al. have developed a general fluorination and subsequent post-treatment approach to achieve heteroatom (nitrogen, sulfur, or boron) “super-doping” for graphene, graphene quantum dots, or single-walled carbon nanotubes with ultrahigh heteroatom contents [32]. However, most of the methods are usually tedious and time-consuming, thus may not suitable for large-scale application.

Aiming at the above mentioned issues, herein, we developed a simple boric acid-assisted strategy to effectively trap nitrogen in B, N co-doped carbon nanosheets (BN-CNSs). Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted great attention in the fields of biosensors, catalysis, and energy storage materials owing to their small particle sizes, abundant edge sites, and various functional groups [34–38]. Using GQDs with an initial nitrogen content of 5.0 at.% as precursors, BN-CNSs show an increased nitrogen content of 7.2 at.% and a boron content of 4.4 at.% due to the balancing of the positive and negative charges in carbon skeleton (Fig. 1 ). This phenomenon is much different from the previous reports that nitrogen content is drastically reduced after thermal treatment of the precursors. As an exemplified application for supercapacitors, BN-CNSs with a surface area of 817 m2 g−1 perform a specific capacitance of 257 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 with high pseudocapacitive contribution owing to abundant active sites by highly doped heteroatoms. They also present a good rate capability of 150 F g−1 at 50 A g−1 as well as remarkable cycle stability. Our work broadens the avenue to design highly-doped functional carbon materials.
GQDs were prepared by a chemical oxidation method using bituminous coal powder (Heishan, Xinjiang, China) as the precursor according to our previous works [39,40].

In a typical process, 0.1 g GQDs and 1.0 g boric acid were fully dispersed into 40 mL deionized water with the assistance of sonication and vigorous stirring. After being dried at 80 °C, the mixture was milled to fine powder and subsequently carbonized at 800 °C for 1 h in N2 atmosphere with the heating rate of 5 °C min−1. BN-CNSs powder was obtained after removing the by-product B2O3 by refluxing in deionized water (100 mL) at 120 °C for 3 h and further washed for several times using deionized water by centrifugation. For comparison, the carbon nanosheets (CNSs) were prepared also using GQDs as carbon precursors and Mg(OH)2 nanosheets as inert templates according to our previous work [39].

The morphology and structure of the samples were measured by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL-2100F). The crystal structure was measured by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance) recorded using a Bruker D8 Advance system operating at 3 kV and Cu Kα radiation. The surface elemental composition was measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250 Xi). The surface area was measured by N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms (ASAP2020, Micromeritics). The pore size distribution was calculated by Non-Local Density Functional Theory (NLDFT) method. The surface area was measured by Braunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. Raman spectrum was recorded on a Bruker SENTERRA dispersive Raman spectrometer with 532 nm wave length incident laser light. The contact angle was measured by contact angle tester (OCAI5EC Dataphysics). The details are seen in our previous works [39,40].

The electrochemical performance of the samples was measured by three-electrode system and two-electrode symmetric supercapacitor type according to our previous studies [40]. The powder sample was completely mixed with acetylene black and polyterfluoroethylene with a weight ratio of 75: 20: 5 in ethanol by sonication to form a slurry, and then coated onto nickel foams (1 × 1 cm2). All the electrochemical measurements were conducted in 6 mol L-1 KOH aqueous electrolyte. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge-discharge tests were measured on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co. Ltd., China). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed with the frequency range from 0.01 to 100,000 Hz by applying AC amplitude of 5 mV. The potential window for three-electrode test was set from −0.9 to 0.1 V vs. Hg/HgO reference. The voltage window for two-electrode test was set from 0 to 1.0 V.

The energy density E (Wh kg−1) and power density P (W kg−1) based on the total mass of active materials in the two electrode cell were calculated according to the following equations:
E =
C s
V
2
8 × 3.6
P =
E t
where
C s
(F g−1) stood for the specific capacitance of single electrode, V (V) represented the voltage window excluding the voltage drop, and t (h) was the discharge time.

In this work, boric acid acts as both boron source and template agent [6]. From the SEM image given in Fig. 2 a, BN-CNSs show a thin-layer lamellar morphology with the lateral size of 2–5 μm. The high magnification image shows a transparent silk-like structure with a uniform thickness of ca. 6 nm (Fig. 2b) [6]. For comparison, we recall our previous work that the GQD-derived CNSs have been prepared using Mg(OH)2 platelets as inert templates at the same carbonization condition [39]. Despite the small lateral size of 0.2–0.4 μm, CNSs exhibit an interconnected loose-stacking structure with similar sheet thickness (Fig. 2c and d). The meso- and macro- pores in between the nanosheets provide abundant active sites, ion migration channels, and electron paths, which are more favorable for capacitive energy storage compared with BN-CNSs. From the HRTEM images, BN-CNSs show an amorphous structure with abundant edge sites due to high heteroatom doping and chemical etching (Fig. 2e). By contrast, CNSs contain some small crystal domains from puzzling of highly crystallized GQDs (marked by arrows in Fig. 2f), revealing the higher conductivity than BN-CNSs.

The peaks located at 24 and 43° in the XRD patterns of samples are identified as the (002) and (100) diffraction peaks of carbon, respectively [40,41]. BN-CNSs exhibit more dispersive diffraction peaks than CNSs, indicating the more disordered structure caused by the highly doped heteroatoms (Fig. 3 a). The bands located at ca. 1350 and 1585 cm−1 in the Raman spectra of the samples are the D- and G-bands, (Fig. 3b) originating from asymmetric vibration of six-member rings and symmetric vibration of CC bonds of carbon skeleton, respectively [6,41]. BN-CNSs show a higher ID/IG ratio of 0.93 than CNSs of 0.91, indicating their higher defect density, which is corresponded to the HRTEM and XRD results. Interestingly, the G-band of BN-CNSs blue-shifts to 1590 cm−1 compared with that of CNSs of 1583 cm−1 (seen in zoomed curves in Fig. 3b), indicating the significantly increased electron concentration of the nanosheets mainly induced by the high concentration negatively charged nitrogen and oxygen atoms [42,43].

The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and corresponding pore size distributions of the samples are shown in Fig. 3c and d. BN-CNSs show a type I isotherm with a rapid adsorption in the low pressure region of P/P0 < 0.1, reaching a well-defined plateau in P/P0 from 0.1 to 0.9, and a small tilt at P/P0 > 0.9, meaning the primary microporous structure centered at 0.5–0.8 nm (Fig. 3d) and some macropores in between the large platelets [44]. CNSs show a type IV isotherm consisting of a rapid adsorption at the low pressure region (P/P0 < 0.1) and an obvious hysteresis loop in P/P0 from 0.4 to 0.95, indicating the hierarchical micro- and meso-porous structure [39,41]. The micropores of the samples are mainly originated from gradual decomposition of the functional groups of the GQD precursors and gas release during carbonization. Therefore, the lower micropore volume of BN-CNSs (0.25 cm3 g−1) than that of CNSs (0.54 cm3 g−1) reveals the trapping of heteroatoms in the nanosheets. CNSs show a higher specific surface area of 1332 m2 g−1 and mesopore volume of 1.00 cm3 g−1 than BN-CNSs of 817 m2 g−1 and 0.37 cm3 g−1 (Table 1 ), which are favorable for capacitive energy storage.

XPS was carried out to investigate the surface chemical composition evolution of the samples, as shown in Fig. 4 and Table 2 . As GQDs are prepared by strong chemical oxidation, they have high oxygen and nitrogen content of ca. 32.0 and 5.0 at.%, respectively. When the inert Mg(OH)2 nanoplates are used as the templates, the oxygen and nitrogen content of CNSs decrease to ca. 12.0 and 2.3 at.% due to decomposition and release of heteroatom-bearing functional groups during high temperature treatment [39]. Interestingly, BN-CNSs possess an increased nitrogen content of 7.2 at.% after carbonization, even higher than the GQD precursors of 5.0 at.%, demonstrating the effective trapping of nitrogen in carbon skeleton and simultaneous releasing of other components (Fig. 4a and Table 2). We also measured the relative contents of C, H, N, and other elements of the GQDs, CNSs, and BN-CNSs by the elemental analysis (Table S1). The results further prove the nitrogen trapping by the boric acid-assisted strategy.

From the XPS C1s spectra (Fig. 4b), BN-CNSs have a similar CO (288.7 eV) content but a much higher C-O/C-N/C-B (286.0 eV) total contents than CNSs [6,44,45]. As the influence of oxygen, nitrogen, and boron on the binding energies of C-O, C-N, and C-B groups are similar, the component contributions are hardly distinguished from the C1s spectrum. For clear understanding, the deconvoluted N1s spectra were given in Fig. 4c. The nitrogen components in GQDs are mainly at high oxidation state containing 4.0 at.% of pyridine-N-oxide (403.5 eV) and 1.0 at.% of pyrrolic N (399.6 eV) because of the strong oxidation during preparation [10,58–60]. Apart from significant release of the nitrogen, some of the pyridine-N-oxide are converted to pyridine N (397.8 eV), pyrrolic N, and graphitic N (400.9 eV) in CNSs after high temperature treatment probably due to the decomposition and carbon reduction reaction [6,44,45]. But when carbonized with boric acid, the contents of pyrrolic and graphitic N significantly increase in BN-CNSs (Fig. 4e). Moreover, a new strong peak appears at 398.7 eV in the N1s spectrum of BN-CNSs, which is identified as the C-B-N component [6,44,45]. The B1s spectrum further confirms the existence of BCO2 and B-N (Fig. 4f) [6,44,45].

Since all the nitrogen components are increased in BN-CNSs, the reason for trapping nitrogen cannot be simply ascribed to the formation of C-B-N bonds. We found that the content of negatively charged oxygen functional groups of BN-CNSs is also slightly higher than that of CNSs (Table 2). The increased electron concentration of BN-CNS nanosheets is also proven by the blue-shifted G-band in Raman spectrum. Accordingly, we propose the possible nitrogen trapping mechanism in terms of reaction energy. Boron and nitrogen are adjacent to carbon in the periodic table. Therefore, substitution carbon atoms by B or N atoms in carbon scaffold would not cause severe lattice distortion, which is favorable from the formation energy aspect. The GQDs with abundant edges and functional groups are highly reactive to boron acid. Moreover, the GQDs in our work are electronegative due to the enriched nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional groups. The doping of electropositive B atoms can help to neutralize the negative charges of carbon skeleton, leading to the formation of B-N bonds, trapping of other nitrogen species such as pyridine N and pyrrolic N, as well as introduction of the active component -BCO2.

The nitrogen contents of doped carbon materials prepared through several common routes are given in Fig. 4g. The precursors (polyacrylonitrile, polydopamine, and polyaniline) with different nitrogen content from 8 to 20 at.% are usually used to obtain nitrogen-doped carbons through pyrolysis [9,10,14,16,18,19] and activation [22,23] process. But the nitrogen contents of the carbon products are usually lower than 4.0 at.% (Table S2). Although NH3 treatment is an effective way to introduce nitrogen into carbon skeleton, the nitrogen content is commonly limited at ca. 5.0 at.% (Table S2) [11,12]. The brief summary clearly demonstrates the uniqueness of our work that the boric acid-assisted method is much effective for trapping nitrogen in carbon skeleton.

As an exemplified application, the electrochemical performance of BN-CNSs and CNSs as supercapacitor electrode materials were investigated using three-electrode system. CNSs show a nearly rectangular CV curve, while BN-CNSs show a distorted curve with a pair of obvious redox peaks located at ca. −0.6 V vs. Hg/HgO reference, as marked by the red box in Fig. 5 a. In addition, the galvanostatic charge–discharge curve of BN-CNSs is also distorted from a triangular shape (Fig. 5b), indicating the combination of electrical double-layer and pseudocapacitive energy storage behaviors of BN-CNSs [6,44,46]. CNSs have a similar oxygen content as BN-CNSs but much lower nitrogen and boron contents. Thus the high pseudocapacitance of BN-CNSs is mainly originated from the trapped nitrogen as well as doped boron. The possible pseudocapacitive reactions for nitrogen species are shown as follows [44,47]:
As for the boron specie -BCO2, all we know is that they show electrochemical activity for pseudocapacitance [44]. But the explicit mechanism needs to be further revealed.

What’s more, the redox peaks in the CV curves of BN-CNSs are still maintained at a high scan rate of 100 mV s−1, revealing the good rate performance of BN-CNSs. From the structural aspect, the small-sized CNSs have a hierarchically porous structure with larger surface area and higher mesopore volume than the microporous BN-CNSs, which are much favorable for fast and high capacitance energy storage. But benefiting from the additional pseudocapacitance provided by the doped heteroatoms, BN-CNSs perform higher specific capacitances of 257, 208, 180, and 150 F g−1 at 1, 10, 30, and 50 A g−1, respectively, than CNSs (Fig. 5e and Fig. S1). The high pseudocapacitive ratio and the edge-enriched amorphous structure of BN-CNSs may lead to the reaction controlled electrochemical kinetics. Thus the capacitance retentions of BN-CNSs at high current densities are not good enough, leading to the similar capacitance of BN-CNSs and CNSs at 50 A g−1.

The electrical double-layer and pseudocapacitive capacitance contributions are distinguished based on the Trasatti method (Fig. S2 and S3) [46]. BN-CNSs perform robust pseudocapacitive behavior at various scan rates (Fig. 5f). The contribution at 10 mV s−1 reaches as high as 48%, leading to a high specific areal capacitance (capacitance per specific surface area) of 0.31 F m−2. This value is not only higher than that of CNSs (0.26 F m−2) [39], but also superior to the theoretical value of graphene (0.21 F m−2) [2] and most of the previously reported carbon materials such as activated carbon (~0.16 F m−2) [48–50], carbon nanosheets (~0.16 F m−2) [51–54], carbon nanotubes (~0.25 F m−2) [55–58], N-doped carbons (~0.19 F m−2) [8,59,60], and co-doped carbons (~0.18 F m−2) [53,61–63] (Table S3).

EIS was used to investigate the electrochemical kinetics of the samples (Fig. 5g). The samples show a similar electric resistance from the intersections with the real axis. BN-CNSs show a smaller semicircle diameter in the high frequency region thus better charge-transfer kinetics than CNSs. The reason is ascribed to that high heteroatom content of BN-CNSs leads to much better surface wettability towards the KOH electrolyte, as proven by the contact angle test shown in Fig. S4 [44]. But from the slopes of the tails in the low frequency region, CNSs show better ion transport kinetics than BN-CNSs owing to the mesoporous structure with sufficient ion migration channels. From the Bode plots given in Fig. 5h, the relaxation time constant (τ0) of BN-CNSs is 0.56 s, which is 2 times lower than 1.21 s for CNSs, indicating better electrochemical kinetics of BN-CNSs primarily coming from good conductivity and high surface wettability.

To further evaluate the potential for practical application, the electrochemical performance of the symmetric supercapacitors using 6 mol L-1 KOH electrolyte is also tested. Although the pseudocapacitive behavior of heteroatom doped carbon materials is commonly observed in three-electrode system, it hardly presents in two-electrode symmetric supercapacitors especially using KOH electrolyte, resulting in the rectangular CV curves like that of CNSs shown in Fig. 6 a and Fig. S5 [5]. Benefiting from the high boron and nitrogen contents as active sites, the as-prepared BN-CNSs also exhibit a distorted CV curve with a pair of redox peaks at ca. 0.2 V, demonstrating obvious pseudocapacitive contribution even for symmetric devices. As shown in Fig. 6 b and c, the IR drops of BN-CNS//BN-CNS supercapacitor at different current densities are all lower than those of CNSs, revealing the smaller equivalent series resistance due to good conductivity and surface wettability [6]. The BN-CNSs//BN-CNSs supercapacitor shows capacitances of 260, 210, 185, and 150 F g−1 at of 1, 10, 30, and 50 A g−1, respectively, which are all higher than those of the CNSs//CNSs counterpart (Fig. 6d). The device exhibits energy densities of 8.75 and 5.80 Wh kg−1 at power densities of 250 and 12,352 W kg−1, respectively, which are better than some of the previously reported materials [44,64–66] (as shown in Fig. 6e). Moreover, no obvious capacitance fading is observed after 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1 (Fig. 6f). We have also lighted the LED lights by 4 serial BN-CNSs//BN-CNSs symmetric supercapacitors (Fig. S6), indicating the application potential.

In summary, we have prepared BN-CNSs with a nitrogen content of 7.2 at.%, a boron content of 4.4 at.%, and a high surface area of 817 m2 g−1 using GQDs with a nitrogen content of 5.0 at.% as the precursors through a boric acid assisted carbonization. Interestingly, BN-CNSs show a higher nitrogen content than the precursor GQDs even after high temperature treatment. The trapping of nitrogen is ascribed to the stabilization of carbon skeleton by balancing the positive and negative charges. Owing to abundant active sites from highly doped boron and nitrogen atoms, BN-CNSs present high capacitance of 257 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, good rate performance of 150 F g−1 at 50 A g−1. Moreover, BN-CNSs show high pseudocapacitance contribution of 48% at 10 mV s−1. The assembled symmetric supercapacitor shows excellent cyclic stability without capacitance fading after 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1. Our work provides in-depth understanding of the synergistic effect of boron and nitrogen atoms for preparing highly doped functional carbon materials.

Jing Li: Writing - original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Yue Dong: Writing - original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Jiayao Zhu: Methodology, Formal analysis. Luxiang Wang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Wenhui Tian: Formal analysis. Jing Zhao: Formal analysis. He Lin: Methodology. Su Zhang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Supervision. Yali Cao: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Supervision. Huaihe Song: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Supervision. Dianzeng Jia: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51702275, 21771157), the National Natural Science Foundation of Xinjiang Autonomous Region (2020D01C019), the Scientific Research Program of the Higher Education Institution of Xinjiang (XJEDU2017S003), Xinjiang Tianshan Xuesong Project (2018XS28), and Xinjiang Tianchi Doctoral Project.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.147239.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in design and fabrication of electrochemical supercapacitors with high energy densities
